text: The surgeon begins the bilateral inguinal hernia repair by creating a large flap across the surgical field. The lower midline diastasis is visible, but no intervention is performed on it. The flap dissection continues across the area, and meshes are laid in place without re-approximating the rectus muscles.
The surgeon dissects medially towards Cooper's ligament while managing the reduction of large sliding hernias.

The surgeon uses three ports for the procedure and introduces a five-millimeter port on the right side for additional retraction assistance. The appendix is visible during the dissection, as the surgeon carefully navigates the plane to cut through fibrous chronic adherence associated with the sliding hernia.

The surgeon exposes the hernia sac, revealing the sliding component of the bowel. Despite the patient being on anticoagulation, the surgical field appears more bloody than expected. Sponges are used to control bleeding while the surgeon searches for the chord structures.

The surgeon receives assistance for finer retraction and dissection, revealing the cord structures at the bottom right or lower midline of the screen. The cord is visibly pulled up onto the hernia sac as it is being extracted.

The surgeon carefully reduces the hernia sac, pulling the cord structures over as they continue dissection. The appendix is visible, having been pulled back into the surgical field. The surgeon ensures no bowel is present in the sac before proceeding, achieving better visualization of the distal extent of the hernia sac. The vas deferens is identified, and proper traction is applied to improve visibility.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the edge of the hernia sac, having completed the right side and checked for a cord lipoma. The focus shifts to the left side, where the sigmoid colon is involved in a sliding indirect hernia with a direct component of pericolic fat. The surgeon identifies and lifts the cord structures laterally with assistance, dissecting them from the lateral aspect of the hernia sac and following the plane into the inguinal canal to fully reduce the sliding hernia.

The surgeon performs inferior dissection while hugging the hernia sac, revealing the vas deferens. After confirming the absence of a cord lipoma on the left side, the surgeon prepares to place two 16x12 anatomic pro grip meshes.

The surgeon secures two meshes to each other, Cooper's ligament, and the anterior abdominal wall. The meshes are positioned to prevent separation due to diastasis. The peritoneal flap is closed by suturing from the middle to each side.
The surgeon closes the right side of the peritoneal flap after completing the closure on the left side.

The surgeon places an angiocath into the surgical space and connects it to suction, effectively evacuating fluid from the area. This step is performed to ensure the mesh remains securely attached to the abdominal wall.
